Name entities only by relation, never by identity. For example, A relates to B; when B and C are active, D is active.

To describe systems and definitions without real referents, entities are referred to with abstract placeholders bound by stated relations rather than by real names. This keeps logic expressible while keeping content referent-free.

This mechanism resolves the tension in definitions and technical or logical documents: the content needs something to talk about, but naming real things would introduce referents. Using relational notation lets a document define and reason about entities purely through their stated relationships. It underpins the deferred logic documents and supports referent-free definitions now.
